title: Deploy an app to Railway
summary: Deploy a Node.js, Next.js, or Docker app to Railway — install the CLI, link the project, provision a Postgres database, set environment variables, and push to production.
declaration: I will deploy this application to Railway. Railway is a cloud platform that supports Node.js, Next.js, Docker, Python, and more, and includes managed databases (PostgreSQL, MySQL, Redis, MongoDB). If you prefer Vercel (Next.js-optimized) or Cloudflare Pages (edge/static), tell me before I begin — Railway is the right choice for apps that need persistent servers, background workers, or managed databases outside of Vercel's infrastructure.
instructions:
## Deploy to Railway

### 0. Prerequisites
- A Railway account at https://railway.app (sign in with GitHub — free tier available).
- A GitHub repository (Railway can also deploy from local directories).
- Node.js >= 18 locally.

### 1. Install the Railway CLI
```bash
npm install -g @railway/cli
```
- Verify: `railway --version`
- Alternative: Homebrew on macOS: `brew install railway`

### 2. Authenticate
```bash
railway login
```
- Opens a browser to complete OAuth with GitHub.
- Alternatively use a token: `railway login --browserless` and paste a token from https://railway.app/account/tokens.

### 3. Initialize the project
```bash
railway init
```
- Creates a new Railway project linked to your current directory.
- Or link to an existing Railway project:
```bash
railway link
```
- Select your team and project from the interactive prompt.
- This creates `.railway/` in the project root — add it to `.gitignore`.

### 4. Provision a PostgreSQL database (if needed)
```bash
railway add --plugin postgresql
```
- Railway provisions a managed Postgres instance and automatically sets `DATABASE_URL` in your Railway environment.
- For other databases:
  - `railway add --plugin mysql`
  - `railway add --plugin redis`
  - `railway add --plugin mongodb`
- To use the database URL locally:
```bash
railway run npm run dev
```
- `railway run` injects all Railway environment variables into the command's process — including `DATABASE_URL`.

### 5. Set environment variables
```bash
railway variables set KEY=value
railway variables set SECRET_KEY=abc123 ANOTHER_KEY=xyz
```
- View current variables:
```bash
railway variables
```
- Bulk import from a `.env` file:
```bash
railway variables import .env.production
```
- Do NOT set `DATABASE_URL` manually if you provisioned a Railway database — it's set automatically.
- Required for most Next.js apps:
```bash
railway variables set NODE_ENV=production
railway variables set NEXTAUTH_SECRET=<secret>
railway variables set NEXTAUTH_URL=https://your-domain.up.railway.app
```

### 6. Configure the start command
- Railway auto-detects Node.js projects and runs `npm start` by default.
- For Next.js, ensure `package.json` has:
```json
{
  "scripts": {
    "build": "next build",
    "start": "next start"
  }
}
```
- Railway runs `npm run build` then `npm run start`.
- For custom commands, create a `railway.json` (or `Procfile`) in the project root:
```json
{
  "$schema": "https://schema.railway.app/railway-schema.json",
  "build": {
    "builder": "NIXPACKS",
    "buildCommand": "npm run build"
  },
  "deploy": {
    "startCommand": "npm run start",
    "healthcheckPath": "/api/health",
    "restartPolicyType": "ON_FAILURE"
  }
}
```

### 7. Deploy
```bash
railway up
```
- Uploads the current directory and triggers a build on Railway's infrastructure.
- Watch build logs in real time:
```bash
railway logs
```
- The app gets a temporary Railway URL: `https://<slug>.up.railway.app`.

### 8. Connect to GitHub for automatic deploys
- Go to https://railway.app → your project → Settings → Source → connect GitHub repository.
- Every push to the main branch triggers an automatic deploy.
- Or from CLI:
```bash
railway connect
```
- This is the recommended production setup — use `railway up` only for one-off deploys.

### 9. Custom domain
```bash
railway domain
```
- Follow prompts to add a custom domain.
- From the Railway dashboard: Settings → Networking → Custom Domain → enter your domain → add the CNAME record to your DNS provider.
- SSL is provisioned automatically.

### 10. Run database migrations
- Migrations should run before the app starts. Add a `release` command in `railway.json`:
```json
{
  "deploy": {
    "startCommand": "npm run start",
    "healthcheckPath": "/"
  }
}
```
- Or run migrations via a one-off command:
```bash
railway run npx prisma migrate deploy
# or for Drizzle:
railway run npx drizzle-kit migrate
```
- For automated migrations on every deploy, add to `package.json`:
```json
{
  "scripts": {
    "start": "npx prisma migrate deploy && next start"
  }
}
```

### 11. Monitor and manage
```bash
railway logs          # stream live logs
railway status        # view deployment status
railway open          # open Railway dashboard in browser
railway shell         # open a shell in the deployed container
```

### Common errors & fixes
- **Build fails: `Cannot find module`** → A dependency is in `devDependencies` but needed at runtime. Move it to `dependencies`, or set `NODE_ENV=development` during build.
- **`ECONNREFUSED` connecting to the database** → `DATABASE_URL` is not set or is pointing to `localhost`. Use `railway run` to inject Railway env vars locally, or verify the variable in the Railway dashboard.
- **Port binding error / app not starting** → Railway injects a `PORT` environment variable. Your app MUST listen on `process.env.PORT`. For Next.js, this is handled automatically. For Express: `app.listen(process.env.PORT ?? 3000)`.
- **Out of memory / OOM killed** → The free tier has 512MB RAM. Upgrade your Railway plan or reduce memory usage (e.g., optimize Next.js bundle size, reduce Prisma connection pool).
- **Deploy succeeds but app returns 502** → The health check path is unreachable. Confirm the app is actually starting on `$PORT`. Check logs: `railway logs`.
- **GitHub auto-deploy not triggering** → Check that the Railway GitHub app has access to the repo. Go to GitHub → Settings → Applications → Railway → Repository Access.
- **`railway login` fails in CI** → Use a service token instead: `railway login --token $RAILWAY_TOKEN` with a token from Railway account settings.

### What NOT to do
- Do NOT hardcode `PORT=3000` — Railway assigns the port dynamically via `$PORT`. Always use `process.env.PORT`.
- Do NOT commit `.railway/` to git — it contains local project linking config and can cause issues on other machines.
- Do NOT store secrets in `railway.json` — use `railway variables set` or the Railway dashboard.
- Do NOT use `railway up` for production deployments in a team — use GitHub auto-deploy so everyone's pushes are tracked and reversible.
- Do NOT run `next start` without first running `next build`. If you skip `npm run build` in the start command, the app will fail.
- Do NOT use the free tier for production apps with SLAs — Railway's free tier has sleep-on-idle and resource limits. Upgrade to a paid plan for production.
- Do NOT provision a new database for every deploy/PR environment unless you need full isolation — use a single shared database for staging and separate for production.
- Do NOT skip the health check configuration. Without a health check path, Railway can't verify your app is running and may mark healthy deploys as failed.
